findings.title: createAnimaOrchestrator is 101 lines (just over 100-line threshold)
findings.description: The createAnimaOrchestrator factory function spans lines 113-213 (101 lines total). Per the severity guide, functions exceeding 100 lines are P1. The function is internally well-structured with named inner functions (handleProcessedContext, handleScreenshotContext) and a clean returned object literal, so the cognitive complexity is lower than the raw line count suggests. The returned AnimaOrchestrator object alone is ~43 lines (lines 169-212) of mostly one-liner method delegates. This is borderline -- confidence is lower because the internal quality partially mitigates the length concern.

findings.title: Near-duplicate PNG buffer generation blocks in test beforeAll
findings.description: Lines 20-32 and 34-46 contain near-identical PNG generation code (13 lines each, 26 total). The only difference is the split condition: `x < width / 2` (vertical split) vs `y < height / 2` (horizontal split). Everything else -- buffer allocation, nested loops, pixel assignment, sharp conversion -- is identical. This is a textbook case of extractable duplication (> 10 lines).

findings.title: Inline time expressions should use named constants
findings.code_snippet:
intervalMs: config.screenshotIntervalMs ?? 60_000,
...
aggregationIntervalMs: config.aggregationIntervalMs ?? 15 * 60 * 1000,
eventRetentionMs: config.eventRetentionMs ?? 3 * 60 * 60 * 1000,
findings.description: Three default time values are expressed as inline arithmetic (60_000, 15 * 60 * 1000, 3 * 60 * 60 * 1000). The test file already defines named constants (TWO_HOURS_MS, FOUR_MINUTES_MS) for the same pattern, showing the codebase prefers named time constants. The production code should follow the same convention for consistency and self-documentation.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE:
intervalMs: config.screenshotIntervalMs ?? 60_000,
aggregationIntervalMs: config.aggregationIntervalMs ?? 15 * 60 * 1000,
eventRetentionMs: config.eventRetentionMs ?? 3 * 60 * 60 * 1000,

AFTER:
const DEFAULT_SCREENSHOT_INTERVAL_MS = 60_000           // 1 minute
const DEFAULT_AGGREGATION_INTERVAL_MS = 15 * 60 * 1000  // 15 minutes
const DEFAULT_EVENT_RETENTION_MS = 3 * 60 * 60 * 1000   // 3 hours

// Then use:
intervalMs: config.screenshotIntervalMs ?? DEFAULT_SCREENSHOT_INTERVAL_MS,
aggregationIntervalMs: config.aggregationIntervalMs ?? DEFAULT_AGGREGATION_INTERVAL_MS,
eventRetentionMs: config.eventRetentionMs ?? DEFAULT_EVENT_RETENTION_MS,

WHY: Named constants are self-documenting. A reader does not need to mentally compute '15 * 60 * 1000 = 900,000ms = 15 minutes'. Also makes it trivial to find and adjust defaults via search.

findings.title: Repeated test setup pattern across 9+ test cases
findings.description: Nine test cases repeat an 8-12 line orchestrator setup block with minor variations (different intimacy scores, different VLM results, different buffers). While test readability benefits from explicit setup, the repetition here goes beyond 'three similar lines' -- it is 8-12 lines repeated 9 times (~90 lines of boilerplate). A factory helper with named options would preserve clarity while reducing noise. Each test also manually calls orchestrator.stop() rather than using afterEach cleanup.

findings.title: AnimaOrchestratorConfig mixes timing, state, and callback concerns
findings.code_snippet:
export interface AnimaOrchestratorConfig {
  screenshotIntervalMs?: number
  aggregationIntervalMs?: number
  inactivityThresholdMs?: number
  eventRetentionMs?: number
  triggers?: readonly TriggerCondition[]
  initialIntimacyScore?: number
  onProactiveResponse?: (event: AnimaProactiveEvent) => void
  onError?: (error: Error) => void
}
findings.description: The config interface has 8 fields spanning three distinct concerns: timing configuration (4 fields), persona state initialization (2 fields), and event callbacks (2 fields). At 8 fields this is manageable now, but as the orchestrator evolves (e.g., adding LLM config, DND schedule config, memory system config), a flat config will become unwieldy. This is a preemptive observation.

positive_observations: Well-structured factory function using closure-based module pattern -- consistent with persona-engine's setupPersonaEngine approach
Clean separation of Imperative Shell (setup-orchestrator.ts with Electron/logging) from orchestration logic (orchestrator.ts with pure function dependencies)
Early return in handleProcessedContext keeps nesting to maximum depth 2
TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP is a clean declarative lookup table rather than a switch statement
Test doubles are properly documented with 'Test Double rationale' comments explaining why each external dependency is stubbed
buildTriggerInput function cleanly documents which fields default to safe values and why (external systems not yet implemented)
Integration test covers the full pipeline end-to-end: screenshot -> VLM -> activity -> trigger -> emotion -> response -> UI event
